title: SEGMENT 18 — Frontier: comparative genomics, behavior at scale, single-cell vertebrates, and AI phenotyping

lines.text: SEGMENT 18 — Frontier: comparative genomics, behavior at scale, single-cell vertebrates, and AI phenotyping
Let me close with a look at where the model-organism field is heading, because the frontier is interesting and unsettled.
Comparative genomics in the Caenorhabditis family.** C. elegans has many close relatives — Caenorhabditis briggsae, Caenorhabditis remanei, Caenorhabditis japonica, and others.
By sequencing and analyzing these closely related species, we can ask: which features of worm biology are general nematode features, and which are quirks of elegans specifically?
Comparative genomics adds a new dimension to model-organism work — instead of one worm, you have a phylogeny of worms, each one a slightly different natural experiment.
Drosophila eclosion mapping and high-resolution behavior.

Modern fly labs are using high-throughput video recording to track every behavior of every fly in a population for days at a time, generating terabytes of behavior data that machine learning algorithms can mine for patterns.
The granularity of behavioral phenotyping has gone from "this mutant doesn't move right" to "this mutant has a 3.7% reduction in grooming bout frequency between 14:00 and 16:00.
" Behavior is becoming a quantitative phenotype on the same scale as gene expression.
Zebrafish single-cell transcriptomics.
The combination of fish embryos (small, transparent, all stages accessible) with single-cell RNA sequencing is producing complete cell-by-cell atlases of vertebrate development.
Every cell of a 24-hour fish embryo, transcriptionally profiled.
The ambition is the Sulston lineage map, scaled to a vertebrate — every cell, every gene, every developmental moment.
We're not there yet, but the fish is the closest we've come to a complete vertebrate developmental atlas.
AI-driven phenotyping.
Across all three organisms, machine learning systems are being trained to recognize subtle phenotypes that humans miss.
A neural network looking at zebrafish embryos can pick up developmental defects that a human screener would call normal.
AI-driven phenotyping is, in effect, a new screening modality — one that can scan millions of animals and pick out the interesting ones automatically.
It's going to make screens of unprecedented scale possible over the next decade.
The big picture: the model-organism enterprise is alive, evolving, and integrating with the broader genomics revolution.
Worms, flies, and fish are not historical artifacts.
They are the active substrate on which we test the functional consequences of every interesting human genetic variant that comes out of the clinic.
As human genetics generates more candidate disease genes, the model organisms become more important, not less — they're the platforms on which those candidates get rapid functional testing.
Brenner, Morgan, Streisinger, Nüsslein-Volhard, Sulston, Kenyon, Fire, Mello — these are the names.
The animals — worm, fly, fish — are the platforms.

The bet they made, fifty to a hundred years ago, that you could understand biology by picking the right simple animal and studying it relentlessly — that bet has paid off beyond what anyone could have imagined.
And the bet is still paying off, every day, in labs around the world.

If you walked into a modern biomedical research building right now, somewhere in the basement there are tanks of zebrafish, somewhere there are bottles of flies, and somewhere there are petri dishes of worms.
The Olympic team is still competing.
Still winning.
Still answering questions nothing else can answer.
That's the model organism story.
Worm, fly, fish.
Three animals.
A century of genetics.
Most of what we know about how life works at the molecular level.
Thanks for staying with me.
Go read some primary literature.
